rationale: Long et al. (2018) is used in the theory development to support a key premise: subjective feelings of understanding can lead people to infer lower risk, even improperly. This is not a passing bibliography mention; it helps motivate the citing paper's core simplicity-risk hypothesis alongside related evidence on risk overgeneralization. The citing paper then moves this logic into a new context (brand simplicity and product/service failure risk) and adds downstream dissatisfaction consequences, indicating a meaningful build-on relation.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Long et al.'s finding that subjective cognitive cues can misguide risk perception, extending that idea from investment risk to consumer judgments of brand failure risk. It introduces perceived brand simplicity and mental-representation dimensionality as the focal mechanism and tests additional post-failure outcomes.
